Curriculum → Caching, asynchrony, and resilience

Module overview

Caching, asynchrony, and resilience

Reduce latency, smooth bursts, and survive failures by placing the right work in caches, queues, and resilient control loops.

5 lessons 0/5 complete Start module

How to navigate it

Use this module like a guided drill

  • Scan the lesson summaries first so you know the progression before opening the first topic.
  • Use the “why it matters” snippets to prioritize the topics that are most likely to surface in interviews.
  • Save the case-study and practice sections for speaking answers out loud after reading the lesson once.

Objectives

What to master here

  • Use cache layers and invalidation strategies responsibly
  • Choose asynchronous patterns to absorb bursty work
  • Design retries, idempotency, and back pressure without making incidents worse

How to use this module

Practice flow

  • Read the summary and explain the core trade-off in your own words.
  • Study the diagram and identify the critical path or failure boundary.
  • Answer the interview prompts aloud before marking the lesson complete.

Interview signal

What reviewers expect

  • Clear assumptions and estimated scale.
  • Justified component choices instead of pattern dumping.
  • Awareness of operations, failure modes, and trade-offs.

Lesson 1

Caching layers and cache placement

Open

Caching can remove repeated work from the critical path, but only if you choose the right layer and understand freshness requirements.

Why it matters

Cache design is a staple interview topic because it touches latency, cost, invalidation, and failure semantics all at once.

What you will cover

Place caches where they save the most workDesign the cache key and TTLPlan for misses and outages

Good interview signal

  • Choose the cache layer based on what work it eliminates.
  • Design cache keys and TTLs explicitly.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 2

Cache invalidation and update patterns

Open

Pick an update pattern that matches read/write mix, freshness requirements, and operational simplicity.

Why it matters

Interviewers often want to hear that you understand the behavior after writes, not only the happy path for cache hits.

What you will cover

Cache-aside is the defaultWrite-through and write-behindRefresh-ahead and explicit invalidation

Good interview signal

  • Name the update pattern after a write.
  • Explain who owns freshness and how stale data is repaired.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 3

Queues, streams, and background work

Open

Move non-interactive or bursty work off the request path so user-facing latency stays bounded and the system can absorb spikes gracefully.

Why it matters

Asynchronous design is essential for email, notifications, indexing, media processing, analytics, and any workload with burstier write traffic than the online path can safely handle.

What you will cover

What belongs off the critical pathQueues vs. streamsOperational controls

Good interview signal

  • Move non-interactive work off the user path.
  • Choose queue or stream semantics intentionally.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 4

Idempotency, retries, and back pressure

Open

Make distributed workflows safe under duplicate delivery, partial failure, and overload instead of assuming every call succeeds exactly once.

Why it matters

These controls separate systems that merely work in demos from systems that remain correct during incidents.

What you will cover

Design for repeated attemptsRetry with restraintBack pressure protects the system

Good interview signal

  • State how duplicate requests are made safe.
  • Use retries with bounded budgets and jitter.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 5

Observability and operational feedback loops

Open

Metrics, logs, traces, and alerting turn architecture choices into something operators can actually trust and improve.

Why it matters

In interviews, observability is often the difference between a design that scales on paper and one that can be debugged in production.

What you will cover

Instrument the golden signalsUse traces for multi-hop requestsClose the loop operationally

Good interview signal

  • Name the most important metrics for the proposed design.
  • Include domain correctness signals, not only infrastructure metrics.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
